turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion of the Court in case 17-130, Lucia v. The Securities and Exchange Commission.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: This case is about the Appointments Clause of the Constitution. That clause requires officers of the United States to be appointed in a certain way; for our purposes today, by a department head. But not every person who works for the federal government is an officer. Some are simply employees and the Constitution has nothing to say about their appointment. The question here is whether Administrative Law Judges in the Securities and Exchange Commission, so let us use some acronyms here, let us call them ALJs in the SEC are officers and so must be appointed in the way the Constitution prescribes. Now, you might wonder why anyone cares enough about that question to bring a lawsuit about it. But there is actually a quite good reason. If an ALJ has not since been appointed in the right way, then he does not have lawful authority to do his job, and if he has been doing his job without that authority, then someone can step up and argue that the things he has done should be voided, wiped off the books, and that is the argument being made here. The petitioner is Raymond Lucia, an investment advisor, who marketed a retirement savings strategy called Buckets of Money. The SEC is the agency charged with punishing fraud by investment advisers. The SEC thought that Lucia's sales pitch was deceitful and brought charges against him. Following its normal procedures, it then assigned an ALJ, again, an Administrative Law Judge within the agency to hear those charges, to preside over a trial and determine whether Lucia committed fraud. The ALJ decided that he did and imposed some serious punishments. But Lucia says that decision should be erased because the ALJ was not properly appointed. Everyone here agrees that he was appointed by other staff members, but Lucia says that SEC ALJs are officers and so they cannot be appointed just by other staff, they have to be appointed by the head of the department. And so Lucia says everything the ALJ did was unauthorized and Lucia's trial has to start over again in front of a different ALJ, who is properly appointed, and we agree. An official qualifies as an officer of the United States if he meets both parts of a two-part test. First, he must hold a continuing office established by law. In other words, his job must be more than occasional or temporary and his office must be described in a statute. Everyone agrees that an ALJ meets that part of the test. The real action here is the test's second part. That requires the official to exercise what this Court has called significant authority. Now, because the significance authority standard is phrased so generally, there are different views on how to determine when it is satisfied. But here we do not have to get into that, because one of our precedents, a case called Freytag v. Commissioner, applied the test to officials who are near carbon copies of the officials we consider today. So all we have to do here is what we did in Freytag. The officials in Freytag were the Special Trial Judges of the Tax Court; they are called STJs. As their name suggests, they conduct trials of people charged with cheating on their taxes. In Freytag we held that they were constitutional officers because they exercised significant authority in presiding over those hearings. In particular, we said they take testimony, conduct trials, rule on the admissibility of evidence and have the power to enforce compliance with discovery orders. That was enough to mean that they had to be appointed by the department head, not any old staff member. The exact same thing is true here. SEC ALJs have all the same powers and do all the same things as the Tax Court's STJs. No less than them, an Administrative Judge of the SEC presides over a trial type proceeding, makes all the rulings that that entails and then issues an opinion about whether the person has committed the crime charged, and if so, what sanctions are appropriate. So we conclude that Administrative Judges of the SEC are officers of the United States and must be appointed in the way the Constitution prescribes. Because the ALJ who conducted Lucia's hearing was not appointed in that way, it gets wiped off the books. He is entitled to a new hearing before a properly appointed new official. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion, in which Justice Gorsuch joins. Justice Breyer has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment in part and dissenting in part, in which Justices Ginsburg and Sotomayor join as to part three. Justice Sotomayor has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justice Ginsburg joins.
